presentation_metadata.title: Swiggy × DPDP Act 2023 — Product Thinking Deck
presentation_metadata.author: Product Manager — Data & Privacy

slides.title: Swiggy asks for all your data with one tap. DPDP says that consent is invalid.
slides.subtitle: Focus: the onboarding consent experience — the first legal touchpoint where personal-data collection is authorized.
slides.problem: Swiggy's onboarding bundles essential delivery data (phone, address) with optional behavioural data (location, marketing, analytics) behind a single 'Accept All'. DPDP Act, Section 6 requires consent that is free, specific, informed, unconditional and affirmative — bundling is none of these.
slides.solution: Swiggy SARAL: unbundled consent buckets, just-in-time micro-notices, a multilingual plain-language layer, an immutable Consent Ledger, and a children's-data gateway.
slides.decision_ask: Approve resourcing + Phase-1 A/B launch to make consent valid without breaking onboarding.
slides.notes: Open with the regulatory countdown. This is a product-architecture problem, not a legal checkbox. Bundled consent is a ticking time bomb for both trust and law.

slides.title: Three people, one broken consent moment
slides.subtitle: The person whose data is at stake — and why the current flow fails each of them.

personas.name: Priya, 28 · Bangalore
personas.model: Gets that Swiggy needs address + phone. Doesn't get why it tracks location when closed, or sends Dineout emails after biryani.
personas.attitude: Shares for clear value; hates dark patterns.

personas.name: Ramesh, 42 · Tier-2
personas.context: First smartphone; weekend family dinners; speaks Kannada.
personas.model: Doesn't read policies; taps 'OK' to reach food faster.
personas.attitude: Trusts by default; needs protecting from rapid-tap habit.

personas.name: Ananya, 16 · Student
personas.context: Snacks after tuition; parent pays via UPI.
personas.model: Unaware her location + browsing is collected; guardian has no visibility.
personas.attitude: N/A — duty of protection is on the platform (S.9).

pain_points: Consent fatigue — 4,000-word policy → abandon or blind-accept
Dark-pattern anxiety — pre-ticked box, 'agree to continue'
No memory — can't recall or revoke consent
Parental blindspot — no mechanism for minors
slides.why: For Priya, bad consent UX = switch. For Ramesh = silent churn. For Ananya's parent = uninstall + social post.
slides.notes: Picture Priya tapping Accept All at 1:15 PM with a meeting in 5 minutes. Zero patience. Any friction = drop-off; any deception = trust erosion.

slides.title: The 'wall of text' onboarding breaks the law before the first order
slides.subtitle: How Swiggy collects data today (stated assumptions) and where it fails DPDP.
slides.diagram_alt: Current bundled onboarding loop vs the SARAL progressive-consent intervention.

gaps.s: No age gate, no parental flow

slides.notes: Pause at the bundled wall — that's where we lose users and break the law. Background location starts before any order.

slides.title: Swiggy SARAL — consent as a product feature, not a legal tax
slides.subtitle: Simple · Accessible · Rational · Actionable · Legal. From all-or-nothing to just-in-time, just-enough, just-for-now.
slides.diagram_alt: Age gate → unbundled buckets → JIT notices → multilingual layer → Consent Manager → Kafka → immutable Ledger → Audit API.

pillars.d: Essential (inline, no opt-out) vs Optional (granular, un-ticked toggles).
Ask at the moment of need + value exchange (₹50 off for WhatsApp alerts).
12 languages, 8th-grade reading level, icon per data category.
Immutable signed log; DPB-auditable in <2 min. Kafka → WORM → Audit API.
Age gate → Guardian Consent Mode; child account runs data-minimal.

slides.notes: SARAL re-architects how Swiggy treats user autonomy. Ledger = insurance. JIT = conversion strategy. Children's gateway = legal shield.

slides.title: One North Star that fuses compliance, conversion and trust
slides.subtitle: Valid Consent Coverage Rate — high VCCR means legally protected AND users aren't bouncing.
slides.diagram_alt: VCCR North Star broken into Valid, Granular, Reach, Trust drivers with guardrails and lagging outcome.

north_star.name: Valid Consent Coverage Rate (VCCR)
north_star.def: % of MAU with explicit, granular, unbundled, ledger-verified consent for every active processing purpose.

success: Compliance: zero audit findings; passes DPB mock audit
Ops: 100% real-time ledger sync, zero data loss / 30d
slides.notes: VCCR is the only metric combining legal safety with engagement. High VCCR but onboarding drops → tighten JIT timing. Low VCCR but onboarding fine → compliance leak.

slides.title: If VCCR drops 15% with no release — a forensic, not a panic
slides.subtitle: Scenario: 3 weeks post-launch VCCR falls 92% → 77%. No release, campaign, or outage.
slides.diagram_alt: Four-branch hypothesis tree: UX regression, cohort shift, backend data drift, external trigger.
analysis_plan: Segment drop by platform / app version / OS → isolate iOS-only vs version bug
Funnel drop-off by consent category → marketing-only (JIT) vs uniform (cohort/external)
Ledger event volume vs app analytics volume → backend data loss
Correlate with acquisition source / campaign ID → cohort shift
Session recordings at consent screens → rage-click / UX confusion
slides.root_cause: Five-Whys + cohort isolation. Example: opt-in fell → JIT fires at checkout → address event mis-mapped to checkout in v8.4.2 → release regression in trigger logic.
next_steps: 0-24h: freeze rollout if <80%, verify pipeline, alert legal
1-7d: A/B JIT timing, hotfix regression, in-app survey
7-30d: re-engage incomplete consent, fix micro-copy, transparency post
slides.notes: The hypothesis tree prevents panic; the analysis plan is a forensic checklist. Key: is it a UX bug, a backend leak, or a market shift?

slides.title: Consent Health Command Center
slides.subtitle: Real-time monitoring of compliance, conversion and trust (mock data, 30-sec rollup).
alerts: VCCR <80% >2h → P0 auto-freeze + page SRE + notify Legal
slides.notes: This dashboard is the war room. Segmentation isolates patient-zero. Under-18 is the biggest blind spot.

slides.title: Phased, validated, reversible — and we never A/B-test illegality
slides.subtitle: From legal foundation to full rollout with hard gates at each phase.
slides.diagram_alt: Four phases over 14 weeks with go/no-go gates leading to compliant, audit-ready state.

ab.handling: For mandatory elements run 'compliance shadow tests': new notice to 100% of a cohort vs a holdback that sees the OLD compliant notice — never a non-compliant one.

validation: Quant: funnel, cohort retention, VCCR trend
Legal: external DPDP mock audit every 2 weeks
Tech: chaos test Ledger (Kafka outage → fallback queue)
slides.notes: Phase 1 is make-or-break. We test HOW to make compliance delightful, never WHETHER compliance is needed. The non-testable list is our red line.

slides.title: Honest accounting of what we trade for compliance
slides.subtitle: We know conversion will hurt and legacy data is messy — the cost of inaction is existential.

risks.t: Accept 3-wk backend extension vs lifetime liability
risks.m: Managed Kafka + WORM S3; 2 senior eng / 8 wks; in-memory fallback

risks.t: 6 mo lower ad revenue for defensible model
risks.m: JIT incentives; first-party personalization; re-engage opt-ins

risks.t: Small top-funnel loss for trust + retention
Higher eng cost for privacy mode
risks.m: Minimal mode: manual address, no marketing, aggregated analytics, blocked sharing

risks.t: Lose some under-18 volume vs S.9 penalty
risks.m: Limited-access order, zero retention, parent education

risks.t: Temp. data-richness loss to cleanse liability
risks.m: 'Trust Refresh' re-consent; purge >12mo profiles; document legitimate use

slides.notes: This is where we prove we're not naive. Conversion will hurt, engineering is hard, legacy is a mess — trade-offs are worth it because the alternative is regulatory shutdown of data ops.

slides.title: Privacy as a product, not a penalty
slides.subtitle: SARAL turns DPDP compliance into a competitive advantage — legal safety, business growth, user trust, at once.

pillars.d: Zero bundled consent · verifiable ledger · parental gateway · audit-ready <2 min
Core onboarding unblocked · opt-in recovered via JIT · premium trust positioning
Plain-language multilingual notices · one-tap revocation · transparent usage

slides.decision_ask: Approve today: (1) 2 senior backend eng + 1 designer + 1 legal PM for 12 weeks; (2) ₹45 lakh for translation, audit & managed infra; (3) Go for Phase-1 A/B on 20 July 2026.

next_steps.a: PRD for Consent Ledger + JIT trigger logic

slides.closing: DPDP isn't a deadline to fear — it's a blueprint for the most trusted food-tech platform in India. Our users' data belongs to them; their trust belongs to us.
slides.notes: End with energy. The ask is specific: people, money, a date. Privacy is not a cost center — it is the product.
